body_txt:
"Kayfabe" is a carny term thought to have originated from the Pig Latin for "be fake", possibly originally by pronouncing it backward ("kay-feeb"). Professional Wrestling adopted the term as a reference to the standard Genre Blindness inherent in the Fourth Wall separating the audience from the action. It is meant to convey the idea that pro wrestling is a genuine sport, these wrestlers are real sportsmen competing for a title and the outcome is totally not predetermined . In most cases of kayfabe, the realization that something is actually an elaborate play and not a real life event may take some people longer than others. But the Willing Suspension of Disbelief is understood for the sake of enjoying the media. Telling the average fan that wrestling is "fake", or that The Muppets aren't real actors, or that their favorite Virtual YouTuber is not a cute anime character but a person using motion capture software, will just make you sound stupid – you're not the first person to say that , and everyone's well-aware by now. But no matter what you say, the fan still likes it. They're participating in the fiction because it's entertaining and WANT to believe in it. This can and often does result in a Loony Fan who isn't aware of the fiction and treats it as Serious Business . In short: Kayfabe is when a work is presenting itself as real, and goes to extremes on and off camera to make it seem believable . The audience plays along not because they think it's real, but because it's more entertaining to think otherwise.
For a singer or band using a fake persona, see Kayfabe Music . See also Flynning , which serves many of the same purposes for depictions of swordplay . For another kind of fiction pretending to be real, see Direct Line to the Author . When actors themselves choose not to break kayfabe in order to better stay in-character, see Method Acting ; if they purposely indulge in their character outside of the studio, it's Adam Westing . When fans jokingly play along with the work as a meme, it's a Play-Along Meme . This can overlap with Contractual Purity if actors are obligated to maintain their personas even in real life. Compare Pro Wrestling Is Real , where wrestling isn't fake within the context of a video game or fictional work. Kayfabe is not to be confused with a Keyblade .
